title: Humboltia phalangifera
taxonomy: Humboldtia phalangifera is a species of plant in the family Apocynaceae. It is native to India and Southeast Asia, and is also known as the 'Malabar Poison Nut'. Further classification of Humboldtia phalangifera is at the genus level, which is Humboldtia. The genus is part of the subfamily Rauvolfioideae. The classification of this species is mainly based on its morphological characteristics and phylogenetic relationships.
uses: Humboldtia phalangifera, a plant native to Southeast Asia, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its roots and leaves are said to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may contribute to its medicinal uses. intro: Humboldtia phalangifera is a plant species native to the tropical forests of the Western Ghats in India, belonging to the family Apocynaceae. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities, contributing to its use in treating various health conditions, such as fever, skin infections, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Humboldtia phalangifera include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its pharmacological activities. In traditional medicine, the plant is commonly prepared into herbal teas, infusions, and extracts, as well as being used in the form of ointments and salves for topical applications.
